options.text: domains are all perfectly aligned

options.explanation: Key concept: At room temperature permanent magnet behaves as a ferromagnetic substance for a long period of time. At room temperature, the permanent magnet retains ferromagnetic property for a long period of time. The individual atoms in a ferromagnetic material possess a dipole moment as in a paramagnetic material. However, they interact with one another in such a way that they spontaneously align themselves in a common direction over a macroscopic volume called domain. Thus, we can say that in a permanent magnet at room temperature, domains are all perfectly aligned.

solution_text: (a) and (d) are correct. Magnetic field lines for magnetic induction (B) form continuous lines so lines of B are necessarily continuous across S. Also magnetic intensity (H) to magnetise varies for inside and outside the lump. So line of H cannot all be continuous across S.

question_text: The primary origin(s) of magnetism lies in atomic currents. Pauli exclusion principle polar nature of molecules. intrinsic spin of electron.
question_html:
The primary origin(s) of magnetism lies in

  1. atomic currents.
  2. Pauli exclusion principle
  3. polar nature of molecules.
  4. intrinsic spin of electron.
solution_text: (a) and (d) are correct. The primary origin of magnetism lies in the fact that the electrons are revolving and spinning about the nucleus of an atom, and we know that a moving charge carries current along with it. We meant this current here as atomic current and which is responsible to produce an orbital magnetic moment. This atomic current gives rise to magnetism. The revolving and spinning about the nucleus of an atom is called intrinsic spin of the electron, which gives rise to spin magnetic moment. So, the total magnetic moment is the sum of orbital magnetic moment and spin magnetic moment.
solution_html:
(a) and (d) are correct.

The revolving and spinning about the nucleus of an atom is called intrinsic spin of the electron, which gives rise to spin magnetic moment. So, the total magnetic moment is the sum of orbital magnetic moment and spin magnetic moment.

question_text: A long solenoid has 1000 turns per metre and carries a current of 1 A. It has a soft iron core of {tex}\mu_{r}={/tex} 1000. The core is heated beyond the Curie temperature, T c . The H field in the solenoid is (nearly) unchanged but the B field decreases drastically. The H and B fields in the solenoid are nearly unchanged. The magnetisation in the core reverses direction. The magnetisation in the core diminishes by a factor of about 10 8 .
question_html:
A long solenoid has 1000 turns per metre and carries a current of 1 A. It has a soft iron core of {tex}\mu_{r}={/tex} 1000. The core is heated beyond the Curie temperature, Tc.

  1. The H field in the solenoid is (nearly) unchanged but the B field decreases drastically.
  2. The H and B fields in the solenoid are nearly unchanged.
  3. The magnetisation in the core reverses direction.
  4. The magnetisation in the core diminishes by a factor of about 108.
solution_text: (a) and (d) are correct. Key concept: The magnetic field intensity H = nl, where n = number of turns per metre of a solenoid and l = current and B = {tex}\mu_{0} \mu_{r} l{/tex} . Also, at normal temperature, a solenoid behaves as a ferromagnetic substance and at the temperature beyond the Curie temperature, it behaves as a paramagnetic substance. We know, for solenoid H = nI. {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} H = 1000 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 1000 Am Thus, H is a constant, so it is nearly unchanged because on heating n and I remain constant. But, {tex}B=\mu_{0} \mu_{r} n I{/tex} {tex}=\mu_{0} n I \mu_{r}{/tex} = k (constant) {tex}\mu_{r}{/tex} but there is a large decrease in the susceptibility of the core on heating it beyond critical temperature, hence magnetic field will decrease drastically. Now, for magnetisation in the core, when temperature of the iron core of a solenoid is raised beyond Curie temperature, then it behaves as a paramagnetic material, where {tex}\left(\chi_{m}\right)_{\mathrm{Ferg}} \approx 10^{3}{/tex} and {tex}\left(\chi_{m}\right)_{\mathrm{Para}} \approx 10^{-5}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\left(\chi_{m}\right)_{\text {Fero }}}{\left(\chi_{m}\right)_{\text {Para }}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{10^{3}}{10^{-5}}{/tex} = 10 8
solution_html:
(a) and (d) are correct.
Key concept: The magnetic field intensity H = nl, where n = number of turns per metre of a solenoid and l = current and B = {tex}\mu_{0} \mu_{r} l{/tex}.
Also, at normal temperature, a solenoid behaves as a ferromagnetic substance and at the temperature beyond the Curie temperature, it behaves as a paramagnetic substance.
We know, for solenoid H = nI.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} H = 1000 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 1000 Am
Thus, H is a constant, so it is nearly unchanged because on heating n and I remain constant.
But, {tex}B=\mu_{0} \mu_{r} n I{/tex}
{tex}=\mu_{0} n I \mu_{r}{/tex}
= k (constant) {tex}\mu_{r}{/tex}
but there is a large decrease in the susceptibility of the core on heating it beyond critical temperature, hence magnetic field will decrease drastically. Now, for magnetisation in the core, when temperature of the iron core of a solenoid is raised beyond Curie temperature, then it behaves as a paramagnetic material, where
{tex}\left(\chi_{m}\right)_{\mathrm{Ferg}} \approx 10^{3}{/tex}
and {tex}\left(\chi_{m}\right)_{\mathrm{Para}} \approx 10^{-5}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\left(\chi_{m}\right)_{\text {Fero }}}{\left(\chi_{m}\right)_{\text {Para }}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{10^{3}}{10^{-5}}{/tex} = 108

question_text: The essential difference between electrostatic shielding by a conducting shell and magnetostatic shielding is due to electrostatic field lines can end on charges and conductors have free charges. lines of B can also end but conductors cannot end them. lines of B cannot end on any material and perfect shielding is not possible. shells of high permeability materials can be used to divert lines of B from the interior region.
question_html:
The essential difference between electrostatic shielding by a conducting shell and magnetostatic shielding is due to

  1. electrostatic field lines can end on charges and conductors have free charges.
  2. lines of B can also end but conductors cannot end them.
  3. lines of B cannot end on any material and perfect shielding is not possible.
  4. shells of high permeability materials can be used to divert lines of B from the interior region.
solution_text: (a), (c) and (d) are correct. Electrostatic shielding is the phenomenon to block the effects of an electric field. The conducting shell can block the effects of an external field on its internal content or the effect of an internal field on the outside environment. For protecting a sensitive equipment from the external magnetic field it should be placed inside an iron cane (magnetic shielding). Magnetostatic shielding is done by using an enclosure made of a high permeability magnetic material to prevent a static magnetic field outside the enclosure from reaching objects inside it or to confine a magnetic field within the enclosure.
solution_html:
(a), (c) and (d) are correct.
Electrostatic shielding is the phenomenon to block the effects of an electric field. The conducting shell can block the effects of an external field on its internal content or the effect of an internal field on the outside environment. For protecting a sensitive equipment from the external magnetic field it should be placed inside an iron cane (magnetic shielding). Magnetostatic shielding is done by using an enclosure made of a high permeability magnetic material to prevent a static magnetic field outside the enclosure from reaching objects inside it or to confine a magnetic field within the enclosure.

question_text: Let the magnetic field on earth be modelled by that of a point magnetic dipole at the centre of the earth. The angle of dip at a point on the geographical equator is always zero. can be zero at specific points. can be positive or negative is bounded.
question_html:
Let the magnetic field on earth be modelled by that of a point magnetic dipole at the centre of the earth. The angle of dip at a point on the geographical equator

  1. is always zero.
  2. can be zero at specific points.
  3. can be positive or negative
  4. is bounded.
solution_text: (b), (c) and (d) are correct. Key concept: Angle of inclination or dip is the angle between the direction of the intensity of the total magnetic field of the earth and a horizontal line in the magnetic meridian. If the total magnetic field of the earth is modelled by a point magnetic dipole at the centre, then it is in the same plane of the geographical equator, thus the angle of dip at a point on the geographical equator is bounded in a range from positive to the negative value.
solution_html:
(b), (c) and (d) are correct.
Key concept: Angle of inclination or dip is the angle between the direction of the intensity of the total magnetic field of the earth and a horizontal line in the magnetic meridian.

If the total magnetic field of the earth is modelled by a point magnetic dipole at the centre, then it is in the same plane of the geographical equator, thus the angle of dip at a point on the geographical equator is bounded in a range from positive to the negative value.

question_text: A proton has spin and magnetic moment just like an electron. Why then its effect is neglected in magnetism of materials?
question_html: A proton has spin and magnetic moment just like an electron. Why then its effect is neglected in magnetism of materials?
solution_text: As we know that magnetic moment of a charged particle of charge e and mass m is {tex}M=\frac{e h}{4 \pi m}{/tex} . Thus, {tex}M_{e}=\frac{e h}{4 \pi m_{e}}{/tex} and {tex}M_{p}=\frac{e h}{4 \pi m_{p}}{/tex} As charge on proton and electron are equal in magnitude, so {tex}\frac{M_{e}}{M_{p}}=\frac{m_{p}}{m_{e}}{/tex} As the mass of proton is 1836 times as of electron, so magnetic moment of proton. {tex}M_{p}=\frac{M_{e}}{m_{p}} m_{e}=\frac{M_{e} \cdot m_{e}}{1836 m_{e}} \quad \Rightarrow M_{p}=\frac{M_{e}}{1836} {/tex} Thus, magnetic moment of proton is {tex}\frac{1}{1836}{/tex} times of electron, so its effect is neglected.
solution_html:
As we know that magnetic moment of a charged particle of charge e and mass m is {tex}M=\frac{e h}{4 \pi m}{/tex}.
Thus, {tex}M_{e}=\frac{e h}{4 \pi m_{e}}{/tex} and {tex}M_{p}=\frac{e h}{4 \pi m_{p}}{/tex}
As charge on proton and electron are equal in magnitude, so {tex}\frac{M_{e}}{M_{p}}=\frac{m_{p}}{m_{e}}{/tex}
As the mass of proton is 1836 times as of electron, so magnetic moment of proton.
{tex}M_{p}=\frac{M_{e}}{m_{p}} m_{e}=\frac{M_{e} \cdot m_{e}}{1836 m_{e}} \quad \Rightarrow M_{p}=\frac{M_{e}}{1836} {/tex}
Thus, magnetic moment of proton is {tex}\frac{1}{1836}{/tex} times of electron, so its effect is neglected.

question_text: Explain quantitatively the order of magnitude difference between the diamagnetic susceptibility of N 2 (~5 × 10 –9 ) (at STP) and Cu (~10 –5 ).
question_html: Explain quantitatively the order of magnitude difference between the diamagnetic susceptibility of N2 (~5 × 10–9) (at STP) and Cu (~10–5).
solution_text: {tex}\rho_{N_2}=\frac{28g}{22.4lit}=\frac{28g}{22400ml}=\frac{28}{22400}g\text{ per }cm^3{/tex} {tex}\rho_{C u}=8 g / c m^{3}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{\rho_{N_2}}{\rho_{Cu}}=\frac{28}{22400\times8}=1.6\times10^{-4}{/tex} Also, {tex}\frac{\chi_{N}}{\chi_{C u}}=\frac{5 \times 10^{-9}}{10^{-5}}=5 \times 10^{-4}{/tex} Magnetic susceptibility, {tex}\chi{/tex} , is given by, {tex}\chi=\frac{\text{Intensity of magnetisation (M)}}{\text{Magnetising field intensity (H)}}{/tex} {tex}\chi=\frac{\frac{\text {Magnetic moment}(M)}{\text {Volume}(V)}}{H}{/tex} {tex}\chi=\frac{M}{H V}{/tex} If m, V, ρ are the mass, volume and density of magnetic material, then {tex}\frac{m}{\rho}=V{/tex} Thus, {tex}\chi=\frac{M \times \rho}{H m}{/tex} So, {tex}\chi{/tex} {tex} \propto {/tex} {tex}\rho{/tex} as {tex}\frac M{mH}{/tex} is constant. So, {tex}\frac{\chi_{N_2}}{\chi_{\mathrm{Cu}}}=\frac{\rho_{{\mathrm N}_2}}{\rho_{\mathrm{Cu}}}=1.6\times10^{-4}{/tex} So, the major difference between diamagnetic susceptibility of N 2 gas and solid Cu is due to their density and is equal to {tex}1.6\times10^{-4}{/tex} .
solution_html:
{tex}\rho_{N_2}=\frac{28g}{22.4lit}=\frac{28g}{22400ml}=\frac{28}{22400}g\text{ per }cm^3{/tex}
{tex}\rho_{C u}=8 g / c m^{3}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{\rho_{N_2}}{\rho_{Cu}}=\frac{28}{22400\times8}=1.6\times10^{-4}{/tex}
Also, {tex}\frac{\chi_{N}}{\chi_{C u}}=\frac{5 \times 10^{-9}}{10^{-5}}=5 \times 10^{-4}{/tex}
Magnetic susceptibility, {tex}\chi{/tex}, is given by,
{tex}\chi=\frac{\text{Intensity of magnetisation (M)}}{\text{Magnetising field intensity (H)}}{/tex}
{tex}\chi=\frac{\frac{\text {Magnetic moment}(M)}{\text {Volume}(V)}}{H}{/tex}
{tex}\chi=\frac{M}{H V}{/tex} 
If m, V, ρ are the mass, volume and density of magnetic material, then
{tex}\frac{m}{\rho}=V{/tex}
Thus, {tex}\chi=\frac{M \times \rho}{H m}{/tex}
So, {tex}\chi{/tex} {tex} \propto {/tex} {tex}\rho{/tex} as {tex}\frac M{mH}{/tex} is constant.
So, {tex}\frac{\chi_{N_2}}{\chi_{\mathrm{Cu}}}=\frac{\rho_{{\mathrm N}_2}}{\rho_{\mathrm{Cu}}}=1.6\times10^{-4}{/tex}
So, the major difference between diamagnetic susceptibility of N2 gas and solid Cu is due to their density and is equal to {tex}1.6\times10^{-4}{/tex}.

question_text: From a molecular viewpoint, discuss the temperature dependence of susceptibility for diamagnetism, paramagnetism, and ferromagnetism.
question_html: From a molecular viewpoint, discuss the temperature dependence of susceptibility for diamagnetism, paramagnetism, and ferromagnetism.
solution_text: The direction of external magnetic field H and magnetism M due to the orbital motion of electrons of diamagnetic substance are opposite so net magnetism becomes zero. Hence, the susceptibility ( {tex}\chi{/tex} ) of diamagnetism is not much affected by temperature. The direction of magnetism due to the orbital motion of electrons in paramagnetism and ferromagnetism material and externally applied field are in the same direction so net magnetism increased and much affected by temperature. As temperature raised the alignment of atomic magnetism is disturbed resulting in a decrease in susceptibility.
solution_html:
The direction of external magnetic field H and magnetism M due to the orbital motion of electrons of diamagnetic substance are opposite so net magnetism becomes zero. Hence, the susceptibility ({tex}\chi{/tex}) of diamagnetism is not much affected by temperature.
The direction of magnetism due to the orbital motion of electrons in paramagnetism and ferromagnetism material and externally applied field are in the same direction so net magnetism increased and much affected by temperature. As temperature raised the alignment of atomic magnetism is disturbed resulting in a decrease in susceptibility.

question_text: A ball of superconducting material is dipped in liquid nitrogen and placed near a bar magnet. In which direction will it move? What will be the direction of its magnetic moment?
question_html:
A ball of superconducting material is dipped in liquid nitrogen and placed near a bar magnet.

  1. In which direction will it move?
  2. What will be the direction of its magnetic moment?
solution_text: i) Away from the magnet ii) The direction of the magnetic moment is opposite to that of the magnetic field applied. Explanation: Now considering both superconducting material and liquid nitrogen are diamagnetic in nature, thereby after dipping the ball in liquid nitrogen it will come out as a diamagnetic material. The ball has acquired diamagnetic properties, when in contact with a magnet will form magnetic poles in opposite direction of the magnet kept thereby, repelling the ball away. As the ball is repelled away it can be said that the magnetic moment will form opposite in direction to the magnetic field applied.
solution_html:
i) Away from the magnet
ii) The direction of the magnetic moment is opposite to that of the magnetic field applied.
Explanation:
Now considering both superconducting material and liquid nitrogen are diamagnetic in nature, thereby after dipping the ball in liquid nitrogen it will come out as a diamagnetic material. The ball has acquired diamagnetic properties, when in contact with a magnet will form magnetic poles in opposite direction of the magnet kept thereby, repelling the ball away.
As the ball is repelled away it can be said that the magnetic moment will form opposite in direction to the magnetic field applied.

question_text: Verify the Gauss’s law for magnetic field of a point dipole of dipole moment m at the origin for the surface which is a sphere of radius R.
question_html: Verify the Gauss’s law for magnetic field of a point dipole of dipole moment m at the origin for the surface which is a sphere of radius R.
solution_text: Let us draw the figure for the given situation, Gauss's law for magnetism is one of the four Maxwell's equations that underlie classical electrodynamics. It states that the magnetic field B has divergence equal to zero, We have to prove that {tex}\oint {/tex} B.dS = 0. This is called Gauss's law in magnetism. Now, the magnetic moment of the dipole at origin O is along the z-axis. Let P be a point at distance r from O and OP makes an angles {tex}\theta{/tex} with z-axis. Component of M along OP = M cos {tex}\theta{/tex} . Now, the magnetic field induction at $P$ due to dipole of moment M cos {tex}\theta{/tex} is {tex}B=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{2 M \cos \theta}{r^{3}} \hat{r}{/tex} From the diagram, $r$ is the radius of a sphere with centre at O lying in yz-plane. Take an elementary area dS of the surface at P. Then, dS = r(sin {tex}\theta{/tex} d {tex}\theta{/tex} ) {tex}\hat{r}=r^{2} \sin \theta d \theta \hat{r}{/tex} {tex}\oint \mathbf{B} \cdot d \mathbf{S}=\oint \frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{2 M \cos \theta}{r^{3}}{/tex} {tex}-\hat{r}\left(r^{2} \sin \theta d \theta \hat{r}\right){/tex} {tex}=-\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{M}{r} \int_{0}^{2 \pi} 2 \sin \theta \cdot \cos \theta d \theta{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{M}{r} \int_{0}^{2 \pi} \sin 2 \theta d \theta{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{M}{2 r}\left(\frac{-\cos 2 \theta}{2}\right)_{0}^{2 \pi}{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{M}{2 r}[\cos 4 \pi-\cos 0]{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{M}{2 r}{/tex} [1 - 1] = 0.
solution_html:
Let us draw the figure for the given situation,

Gauss's law for magnetism is one of the four Maxwell's equations that underlie classical electrodynamics. It states that the magnetic field B has divergence equal to zero, We have to prove that {tex}\oint {/tex} B.dS = 0. This is called Gauss's law in magnetism.
Now, the magnetic moment of the dipole at origin O is along the z-axis.
Let P be a point at distance r from O and OP makes an angles {tex}\theta{/tex} with z-axis.
Component of M along OP = M cos {tex}\theta{/tex}.
Now, the magnetic field induction at $P$ due to dipole of moment  M cos {tex}\theta{/tex} is {tex}B=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{2 M \cos \theta}{r^{3}} \hat{r}{/tex}
From the diagram, $r$ is the radius of a sphere with centre at O lying in yz-plane.
Take an elementary area dS of the surface at P. Then,
dS = r(sin {tex}\theta{/tex} d{tex}\theta{/tex}) {tex}\hat{r}=r^{2} \sin \theta d \theta \hat{r}{/tex}
{tex}\oint \mathbf{B} \cdot d \mathbf{S}=\oint \frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{2 M \cos \theta}{r^{3}}{/tex} {tex}-\hat{r}\left(r^{2} \sin \theta d \theta \hat{r}\right){/tex}
{tex}=-\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{M}{r} \int_{0}^{2 \pi} 2 \sin \theta \cdot \cos \theta d \theta{/tex}
{tex}=-\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{M}{r} \int_{0}^{2 \pi} \sin 2 \theta d \theta{/tex}
{tex}=-\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{M}{2 r}\left(\frac{-\cos 2 \theta}{2}\right)_{0}^{2 \pi}{/tex}
{tex}=-\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{M}{2 r}[\cos 4 \pi-\cos 0]{/tex}
{tex}=-\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{M}{2 r}{/tex} [1 - 1] = 0.

solution_text: The poles must be symmetric to each other or a magnet. Suppose we want to verify the analogy between electrostatic and magnetostatic by an explicit experiment. Consider the motion of

solution_text: Electric field {tex}(\vec{E}){/tex} and magnetic field {tex}(\vec{B}){/tex} are related as Key concept: Suppose the angle between {tex}\vec{\mathbf{p}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} is {tex}\theta{/tex} . question_text: A bar magnet of the magnetic moment {tex}\vec{m}{/tex} and moment of inertia I (about centre, perpendicular to length) is cut into two equal pieces, perpendicular to the length. Let T be the period of oscillations of the original magnet about an axis through the midpoint, perpendicular to the length, in a magnetic field B. What would be the similar period T′ for each piece?
question_html: A bar magnet of the magnetic moment {tex}\vec{m}{/tex} and moment of inertia I (about centre, perpendicular to length) is cut into two equal pieces, perpendicular to the length. Let T be the period of oscillations of the original magnet about an axis through the midpoint, perpendicular to the length, in a magnetic field B. What would be the similar period T′ for each piece?
solution_text: The magnetic moment of each part of magnet {tex}M^{\prime}=\frac{M}{2}{/tex} If m is the mass of the original magnet, then the mass of each two magnets m’ will be {tex}\frac{m}{2}{/tex} . The length of the new magnet = {tex}l^{\prime}=\frac{l}{2}{/tex} Now, the moment of inertia, {tex}I=\frac{m l^{2}}{12}{/tex} and time period T = {tex}2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{I}{M B}}{/tex} Thus, {tex}\frac{T}{T^{\prime}}=\frac{2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{I}{M B}}}{2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{I^{\prime}}{M^{\prime} B}}}=\sqrt{\frac{I}{M} \cdot \frac{M^{\prime}}{I^{\prime}}}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{T'}{T}=\sqrt{\frac{M}{M^{\prime}} \cdot \frac{I^{\prime}}{I}}{/tex} Now, {tex}\frac{I^{\prime}}{I}=\frac{\frac{m^{\prime} l'^{2}}{12}}{\frac{m l^{2}}{12}}=\frac{\frac{m}{2} \cdot\left(\frac{l}{2}\right)^{2}}{m l^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{I^{\prime}}{I}=\frac{\frac{m}{2} \cdot \frac{l^{2}}{4}}{m l^{2}}=\frac{1}{8}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{M}{M^{\prime}}=\frac{M}{\frac{M}{2}}=\frac{2}{1}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{T'}{T}=\sqrt{\frac{2}{1} \times \frac{1}{8}}=\sqrt{\frac{1}{4}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{T'}{T}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} or {tex}T^{\prime}=\frac{T}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
The magnetic moment of each part of magnet {tex}M^{\prime}=\frac{M}{2}{/tex}
If m is the mass of the original magnet, then the mass of each two magnets m’ will be {tex}\frac{m}{2}{/tex}.
The length of the new magnet = {tex}l^{\prime}=\frac{l}{2}{/tex}
Now, the moment of inertia,
{tex}I=\frac{m l^{2}}{12}{/tex}
and time period T = {tex}2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{I}{M B}}{/tex}
Thus,
{tex}\frac{T}{T^{\prime}}=\frac{2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{I}{M B}}}{2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{I^{\prime}}{M^{\prime} B}}}=\sqrt{\frac{I}{M} \cdot \frac{M^{\prime}}{I^{\prime}}}{/tex} or  {tex}\frac{T'}{T}=\sqrt{\frac{M}{M^{\prime}} \cdot \frac{I^{\prime}}{I}}{/tex}
Now, {tex}\frac{I^{\prime}}{I}=\frac{\frac{m^{\prime} l'^{2}}{12}}{\frac{m l^{2}}{12}}=\frac{\frac{m}{2} \cdot\left(\frac{l}{2}\right)^{2}}{m l^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{I^{\prime}}{I}=\frac{\frac{m}{2} \cdot \frac{l^{2}}{4}}{m l^{2}}=\frac{1}{8}{/tex}
and {tex}\frac{M}{M^{\prime}}=\frac{M}{\frac{M}{2}}=\frac{2}{1}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{T'}{T}=\sqrt{\frac{2}{1} \times \frac{1}{8}}=\sqrt{\frac{1}{4}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{T'}{T}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} or {tex}T^{\prime}=\frac{T}{2}{/tex}

question_text: Use the Ampere’s law for {tex}\vec H{/tex} and continuity of lines of {tex}\vec B{/tex} , to conclude that inside a bar magnet, lines of run from the N-pole to S-pole, while lines of must run from the S-pole to N-pole.
question_html:
Use the Ampere’s law for {tex}\vec H{/tex} and continuity of lines of {tex}\vec B{/tex}, to conclude that inside a bar magnet,

  1. lines of run from the N-pole to S-pole, while
  2. lines of must run from the S-pole to N-pole.
solution_text: Consider an Ampere loop C inside and outside the magnet NS on side PQ of magnet then {tex}\int_{P}^{Q} \vec{H} \cdot \vec{d l}=\int_{Q}^{P} \frac{\vec{B}}{\mu_{0}} \vec{d l}{/tex} Where B is magnetic field and m 0 is dipole moment. As angle between B and dl varies from 90 o , So {tex}\int_{P}^{Q} \vec{H} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\int_{Q}^{P} \frac{\vec{B}}{\mu_{0}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}>0{/tex} i.e. positive. Hence, the value of B must be varied from south pole to north pole inside the magnet. According to Ampere's law, we get {tex}\oint_{P D P} \vec{H} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=0{/tex} {tex}\oint_{P Q P} \vec{H} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\int_{P}^{Q} \vec{H} \cdot d \vec{l}+\int_{Q}^{P} \vec{H} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=0{/tex} As {tex}\int_{p}^{Q} H \cdot d l>0{/tex} (outside the magnet) and {tex}\int_{0}^{P} H \cdot d l<0{/tex} (inside the magnet). It is due to the angle between H and dl is more than 90 o inside the magnet so cos {tex}\theta{/tex} is negative. It means the lines of H must run from north pole to south pole inside the bar magnet.
solution_html:
Consider an Ampere loop C inside and outside the magnet NS on side PQ of magnet then
{tex}\int_{P}^{Q} \vec{H} \cdot \vec{d l}=\int_{Q}^{P} \frac{\vec{B}}{\mu_{0}} \vec{d l}{/tex}
Where B is magnetic field and m0 is dipole moment. As angle between B and dl varies from 90o, So {tex}\int_{P}^{Q} \vec{H} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\int_{Q}^{P} \frac{\vec{B}}{\mu_{0}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}>0{/tex}  i.e. positive.
Hence, the value of B must be varied from south pole to north pole inside the magnet.
According to Ampere's law, we get  {tex}\oint_{P D P} \vec{H} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=0{/tex}
{tex}\oint_{P Q P} \vec{H} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\int_{P}^{Q} \vec{H} \cdot d \vec{l}+\int_{Q}^{P} \vec{H} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=0{/tex}
As {tex}\int_{p}^{Q} H \cdot d l>0{/tex} (outside the magnet) and {tex}\int_{0}^{P} H \cdot d l<0{/tex} (inside the magnet). It is due to the angle between H and dl is more than 90o inside the magnet so cos {tex}\theta{/tex} is negative. It means the lines of H must run from north pole to south pole inside the bar magnet.

Magnetic field induction at a point distance r from the magnetic dipole of moment is {tex}|B|=\frac{\mu_{0} 2|M|}{4 \pi z^3}=\frac{\mu_{0} M}{2 \pi z^{3}}{/tex} Along z-axis from P to Q, {tex}\int_{p}^{Q}{/tex} B. dl = {tex}\int_{p}^{Q}{/tex} B. dl cos 0° = {tex}\int_{a}^{R}{/tex} B dz {tex}=\int_{a}^{R} \frac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \frac{M}{z^{3}} d z{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\mu_{0} M}{2 \pi}\left(\frac{-1}{2}\right)\left(\frac{1}{R^{2}}-\frac{1}{a^{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{\mu_{0} M}{4 \pi}\left(\frac{1}{a^{2}}-\frac{1}{R^{2}}\right){/tex} Along the quarter circle QS of radius R is given in the figure below: The point A lies on the equatorial line of the magnetic dipole of moment M sin {tex}\theta{/tex} Magnetic filed at point A on the circular arc is {tex}\therefore \quad B_{}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{M \sin \theta}{R^{3}} ; d l=R d \theta{/tex} {tex}\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} B_{} d l \cos 0^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}+\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} B_{} d l \cos 90^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2}\left(\frac{\mu_{0} m}{4 \pi R^{3}}\right) \sin \theta(R d \theta){/tex} {tex}=\frac{\mu_{0} m}{4 \pi R^{2}} \int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \sin{/tex} {tex}\theta d \theta=\frac{\mu_{0} m}{4 \pi R^{2}}{/tex} Along x-axis over the path ST, consider the figure given ahead From figure, every point lies on the equatorial line of magnetic dipole. Magnetic field induction at a point distance x from the dipole is {tex}B=-\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{M}{x^{3}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \int_{S}^{T} B \cdot d l{/tex} {tex}=\int_{R}^{a}-\frac{\mu_{0} M}{4 \pi x^{3}} \cdot d l=0{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} angle between (-M) and dl is 90°] Along the quarter circle TP of radius a. Consider the figure given below: From case (b), we get line integral of B along the quarter circle TP of radius a is circular arc TP radius a is circular are TP. {tex}\int B \cdot d l=\int_{\pi / 2}^{0} \frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{M \sin \theta}{a^{3}} a d \theta{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{M}{a^{2}} \int_{\pi / 2}^{0} \sin \theta d \theta{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{M}{a^{2}}[-\cos \theta]_{\pi / 2}^{0}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{-\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{M}{a^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \oint_{P Q S T} B \cdot d l=\int_{P}^{Q} B \cdot d l{/tex} {tex}+\int_{0}^{S} B \cdot d l+\int_{S}^{T} B \cdot d l{/tex} {tex}+\int_{T}^{P} B \cdot d l{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\mu_{0} M}{4 \pi}\left[\frac{1}{a^{2}}-\frac{1}{R^{2}}\right]{/tex} {tex}+\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{M}{R^{2}}+0+\left(-\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{M}{a^{2}}\right)=0{/tex} these are the required result

question_text: What are the dimensions of {tex}\chi{/tex} , the magnetic susceptibility? Consider an H-atom. Guess an expression for {tex}\chi{/tex} , upto a constant by constructing a quantity of dimensions of {tex}\chi{/tex} , out of parameters of the atom: e, m, v, R and {tex}\mu_{0}{/tex} . Here, m is the electronic mass, v is electronic velocity, R is Bohr radius. Estimate the number so obtained and compare with the value of {tex}|\chi| \sim 10^{-5}{/tex} for many solid materials.
question_html: What are the dimensions of {tex}\chi{/tex}, the magnetic susceptibility? Consider an H-atom. Guess an expression for {tex}\chi{/tex}, upto a constant by constructing a quantity of dimensions of {tex}\chi{/tex}, out of parameters of the atom: e, m, v, R and {tex}\mu_{0}{/tex}. Here, m is the electronic mass, v is electronic velocity, R is Bohr radius. Estimate the number so obtained and compare with the value of {tex}|\chi| \sim 10^{-5}{/tex} for many solid materials.
solution_text: Key concept: Magnetic susceptibility: It is the property of the substance which shows how easily a substance can be magnetised. A related term is magnetizability, the proportion between magnetic moment and magnetic flux density. It can also be defined as the ratio of intensity of magnetisation (I) in a substance to the magnetic intensity (H) applied to the substance, i.e. {tex}\chi_{m}=\frac{I}{H}{/tex} {tex}\chi_{m}=\frac{I}{H}=\frac{(\text { intensity of magnetisation })}{(\text { Magnetising force })}{/tex} As I and H both have same units and dimensions, hence {tex}\chi{/tex} has no dimensions. In the problem, {tex}\chi{/tex} is related with e,m, v, R and {tex}\mu_{0}{/tex} . We know that dimensions of {tex}\mu_{0}{/tex} [MLT -2 A -2 ] From Biot-Savart's law, {tex}d B=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{I d l \sin \theta}{r^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mu_{0}=\frac{4 \pi r^{2} d B}{I d l \sin \theta}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{4 \pi r^{2}}{I d l \sin \theta} \times \frac{f}{q v \sin \theta}{/tex} {tex}\left[\because d B=\frac{F}{q v \sin \theta}\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Dimensions of {tex}\mu_{0}=\frac{L^{2} \times\left(M L T^{-2}\right)}{\left(Q T^{-1}\right)(L) \times 1 \times(Q)\left(L T^{-1}\right) \times(1)}{/tex} = [MLQ -2 ] where, Q is the dimensions of charge. This is the required result. As {tex}\chi{/tex} is dimensionless, it should have no involvement of charge Q in its dimensional formula. It will be so if {tex}\mu_{0}{/tex} and e together should have the value {tex}\mu_{0} e^{2}{/tex} , as e has the dimensions of charge, Let, {tex}\chi=\mu_{0} e^{2} m^{a} v^{b} R^{c}{/tex} ...(i) where a, b, c are the power of m, v and R respectively, such that relation (i) is satisfied. Dimensional equation of (i) is [M 0 L 0 T 0 A 0 T 0 ] = [MLA - 2 T -2 ] {tex}\times{/tex} [A 2 T 2 ] [M] a {tex}\times{/tex} [LT -1 ] b {tex}\times{/tex} [L] c = [M I+a + L I+b+c T -b A 0 ] Equating the powers of M, L and T, we get 0 = 1 + a {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = -1, 0 = 1 + b + c ...(ii) 0 = -b {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b = 0, 0 = 1 + 0 + c or c = -1 Putting values in Eq. (i), we get {tex}\chi=\mu_{0} e^{2} m^{-1} v^{0} R^{-1}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\mu_{0} e^{2}}{m R}{/tex} Here, {tex}\mu_{0}=4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{Tm} \mathrm{A}^{-1}{/tex} e = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 C m = 9.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -31 kg, R = 10 -10 m {tex}\chi=\frac{\left(4 \pi \times 10^{-7}\right) \times\left(1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right)^{2}}{\left(9.1 \times 10^{-31}\right) \times 10^{-10}}=10^{-4}{/tex} {tex}\chi \over \chi_{given solid}{/tex} = {tex}10^{-4}\over10^{-5}{/tex} =10
solution_html:
Key concept: Magnetic susceptibility: It is the property of the substance which shows how easily a substance can be magnetised. A related term is magnetizability, the proportion between magnetic moment and magnetic flux density. It can also be defined as the ratio of intensity of magnetisation (I) in a substance to the magnetic intensity (H) applied to the substance, i.e. {tex}\chi_{m}=\frac{I}{H}{/tex}
{tex}\chi_{m}=\frac{I}{H}=\frac{(\text { intensity of magnetisation })}{(\text { Magnetising force })}{/tex}
As I and H both have same units and dimensions, hence {tex}\chi{/tex} has no dimensions. In the problem, {tex}\chi{/tex} is related with e,m, v, R and {tex}\mu_{0}{/tex}. We know that dimensions of {tex}\mu_{0}{/tex} [MLT-2 A-2]
From Biot-Savart's law,
{tex}d B=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{I d l \sin \theta}{r^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mu_{0}=\frac{4 \pi r^{2} d B}{I d l \sin \theta}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{4 \pi r^{2}}{I d l \sin \theta} \times \frac{f}{q v \sin \theta}{/tex} {tex}\left[\because d B=\frac{F}{q v \sin \theta}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Dimensions of
{tex}\mu_{0}=\frac{L^{2} \times\left(M L T^{-2}\right)}{\left(Q T^{-1}\right)(L) \times 1 \times(Q)\left(L T^{-1}\right) \times(1)}{/tex} = [MLQ-2]
where, Q is the dimensions of charge. This is the required result.
As {tex}\chi{/tex} is dimensionless, it should have no involvement of charge Q in its dimensional formula. It will be so if {tex}\mu_{0}{/tex} and e together should have the value {tex}\mu_{0} e^{2}{/tex}, as e has the dimensions of charge,
Let, {tex}\chi=\mu_{0} e^{2} m^{a} v^{b} R^{c}{/tex} ...(i)
where a, b, c are the power of m, v and R respectively, such that relation (i) is satisfied.
Dimensional equation of (i) is
[M0L0T0A0T0] = [MLA - 2 T-2] {tex}\times{/tex} [A2T2] [M]a {tex}\times{/tex} [LT-1]b {tex}\times{/tex} [L]c
= [MI+a + LI+b+c T-b A0]
Equating the powers of M, L and T, we get
0 = 1 + a  {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = -1, 0 = 1 + b + c ...(ii)
0 = -b {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b = 0, 0 = 1 + 0 + c or c = -1
Putting values in Eq. (i), we get
{tex}\chi=\mu_{0} e^{2} m^{-1} v^{0} R^{-1}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\mu_{0} e^{2}}{m R}{/tex}
Here, {tex}\mu_{0}=4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{Tm} \mathrm{A}^{-1}{/tex}
e = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 C
m = 9.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-31 kg, R = 10-10 m
{tex}\chi=\frac{\left(4 \pi \times 10^{-7}\right) \times\left(1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right)^{2}}{\left(9.1 \times 10^{-31}\right) \times 10^{-10}}=10^{-4}{/tex}

{tex}\chi \over \chi_{given solid}{/tex} ={tex}10^{-4}\over10^{-5}{/tex}=10

Assume the dipole model for earth’s magnetic field B  which is given by BV = vertical component of magnetic field {tex}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{2 m \cos \theta}{r^{3}}{/tex} BH = Horizontal component of magnetic field {tex}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{\sin \theta m}{r^{3}}{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} = 90° - lattitude as measured from magnetic equator. Find loci of points for which

  1. {tex}|\mathbf{\vec B}|{/tex} is minimum;

solution_text: {tex}B_{V}=\frac{\mu_{0} 2 m \cos \theta}{4 \pi}{/tex} {tex}B_{H}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{m \sin \theta}{r^{3}}{/tex} These are the components of B to net magnetic field will be {tex}\tan \delta=\frac{B_{V}}{B_{H}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\mu_{0} m}{4 \pi r^{3}}{/tex} {tex}\left[3 \cos ^{2} \theta+1\right]^{1 / 2}{/tex} From above equation, the value of B is minimum, if cos {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} . Thus, B is minimum at the magnetic equator. Angle of dip, {tex}\tan \delta=\frac{B_{V}}{B_{H}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{2 m \cos \theta}{r^{3}}}{\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{\sin \theta \cdot m}{r^{3}}}=2 \cot \theta{/tex} ...(i) {tex}\tan \delta=2 \cot \theta{/tex} For dip angle is zero i.e., {tex}\delta=0{/tex} cot {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0 {tex}\theta=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} For this value of {tex}\theta{/tex} angle of dip is vanished. It means that locus is again magnetic equator. {tex}\tan \delta=\frac{B_{V}}{B_{H}}{/tex} Angle of dip i.e., {tex}\delta=\pm 45^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}\frac{B_{V}}{B_{H}}=\tan \left(\pm 45^{\circ}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{B_{V}}{B_{H}}=1{/tex} {tex}2 \cot \theta=1{/tex} [From Eq. (i)] {tex}\cot \theta=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\tan \theta=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} = tan -1 (2) Thus, {tex}\theta{/tex} = tan -1 (2) is the locus.

1. {tex}B_{V}=\frac{\mu_{0} 2 m \cos \theta}{4 \pi}{/tex}
    {tex}B_{H}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{m \sin \theta}{r^{3}}{/tex}

question_text: Consider the plane S formed by the dipole axis and the axis of the earth. Let P be a point on the magnetic equator and in S. Let Q be the point of intersection of the geographical and magnetic equators. Obtain the declination and dip angles at P and Q.
question_html: Consider the plane S formed by the dipole axis and the axis of the earth. Let P be a point on the magnetic equator and in S. Let Q be the point of intersection of the geographical and magnetic equators. Obtain the declination and dip angles at P and Q.
solution_text: The point P is on the magnetic equator, so the angle of dip is zero. Also, point Q is on the magnetic equator, so the angle of dip is zero. Also, point P is in the plane S, and the needle is in the North, so the angle of declination at P is zero. Now, Q is the point of intersection between the geographical meridian and magnetic meridian. The angle between the axis of rotation of the earth and the magnetic axis is {tex}11.3^{\circ}{/tex} , so the angle of declination at Q is 11.3 o .
solution_html:
The point P is on the magnetic equator, so the angle of dip is zero. Also, point Q is on the magnetic equator, so the angle of dip is zero.
Also, point P is in the plane S, and the needle is in the North, so the angle of declination at P is zero.
Now, Q is the point of intersection between the geographical meridian and magnetic meridian. The angle between the axis of rotation of the earth and the magnetic axis is {tex}11.3^{\circ}{/tex}, so the angle of declination at Q is 11.3o.

question_text: There are two current carrying planar coils made each from identical wires of length L. C 1 is circular (radius R) and C 2 is square (side a). They are so constructed that they have same frequency of oscillation when they are placed in the same uniform {tex}\vec B{/tex} and carry the same current. Find a in terms of R.
question_html: There are two current carrying planar coils made each from identical wires of length L. C1 is circular (radius R) and C2 is square (side a). They are so constructed that they have same frequency of oscillation when they are placed in the same uniform {tex}\vec B{/tex} and carry the same current. Find a in terms of R.
solution_text: According to the problem, there are two current carrying planar coils made each from identical wires of length L.C 1 = circular coil of radius R, length L, number of turns per unit length {tex}n_{1}=\frac{L}{2 \pi R}{/tex} C 2 = square of side a and perimeter L, number of turns per unit length n 2 {tex}=\frac{L}{4 a}{/tex} Magnetic moment of C 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} m 1 = n 1 iA 1 (where i is the current in the coil) Magnetic moment of C 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} m 2 = n 2 iA 2 (where i is the current in the coil) {tex}m_{1}=\frac{L \cdot i \cdot \pi R^{2}}{2 \pi R}{/tex} {tex}m_{2}=\frac{L}{4 a} \cdot i \cdot a^{2}{/tex} {tex}m_{1}=\frac{L i R}{2}{/tex} {tex}m_{2}=\frac{L i a}{4}{/tex} Moment of inertia of C 1 {tex}\Rightarrow I_{1}=\frac{M R^{2}}{2}{/tex} Moment of inertia of C 2 coil is given by {tex}\Rightarrow I_{2}=\frac{M a^{2}}{12}{/tex} ...(ii) (where M is the mass of coil) Frequency of C 1 {tex}\Rightarrow f_{1}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{I_{1}}{m_{1} B}}{/tex} ...(iii) Frequency of C 2 {tex}\Rightarrow f_{2}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{I_{2}}{m_{2} B}}{/tex} ...(iv) According to problem, f 1 = f 2 {tex}2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{I_{1}}{m_{1} B}}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{I_{2}}{m_{2} B}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{I_{1}}{m_{1}}=\frac{I_{2}}{m_{2}} {/tex} or {tex}\frac{m_{2}}{m_{1}}=\frac{I_{2}}{I_{1}}{/tex} Substituting the values from Eqs. (i), (ii), (iii) and (iv) (and also substitute the values of m 1 and m 2 in equation (iii) and (iv)) {tex}\frac{L i a \cdot 2}{4 \times L i R}=\frac{M a^{2} \cdot 2}{12 \cdot M R^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{a}{2 R}=\frac{a^{2}}{6 R^{2}}{/tex} 3R = a Thus, the value of a is 3R.
solution_html:
According to the problem, there are two current carrying planar coils made each from identical wires of length L.C1 = circular coil of radius R, length L, number of turns per unit length
{tex}n_{1}=\frac{L}{2 \pi R}{/tex}
C2 = square of side a and perimeter L, number of turns per unit length n2 {tex}=\frac{L}{4 a}{/tex}

Magnetic moment of C1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} m1 = n1 iA1 (where i is the current in the coil)
Magnetic moment of C2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} m2 = n2 iA2 (where i is the current in the coil)
{tex}m_{1}=\frac{L \cdot i \cdot \pi R^{2}}{2 \pi R}{/tex}
{tex}m_{2}=\frac{L}{4 a} \cdot i \cdot a^{2}{/tex}
{tex}m_{1}=\frac{L i R}{2}{/tex}
{tex}m_{2}=\frac{L i a}{4}{/tex}
Moment of inertia of C1 {tex}\Rightarrow I_{1}=\frac{M R^{2}}{2}{/tex}
Moment of inertia of C2  coil is given by  {tex}\Rightarrow I_{2}=\frac{M a^{2}}{12}{/tex} ...(ii) (where M is the mass of coil)
Frequency of C1 {tex}\Rightarrow f_{1}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{I_{1}}{m_{1} B}}{/tex} ...(iii)
Frequency of C2 {tex}\Rightarrow f_{2}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{I_{2}}{m_{2} B}}{/tex} ...(iv)
According to problem, f1 = f2
{tex}2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{I_{1}}{m_{1} B}}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{I_{2}}{m_{2} B}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{I_{1}}{m_{1}}=\frac{I_{2}}{m_{2}} {/tex} or {tex}\frac{m_{2}}{m_{1}}=\frac{I_{2}}{I_{1}}{/tex}
Substituting the values from Eqs. (i), (ii), (iii) and (iv) (and also substitute the values of m1 and m2 in equation (iii) and (iv))
{tex}\frac{L i a \cdot 2}{4 \times L i R}=\frac{M a^{2} \cdot 2}{12 \cdot M R^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{a}{2 R}=\frac{a^{2}}{6 R^{2}}{/tex}
3R = a
Thus, the value of a is 3R.
